prompt: Fan static pressure measurements above design in existing VAV systems may indicate:

options.text: Dirty filters, closed dampers, undersized ducts, or stuck VAV boxes
Perfect duct balance with no remediation needed
Excess free cooling from economizers
Reduced fan energy consumption

explanation: Excess static drives fan kW and may reduce airflow to zones.

distractorExplanations.b: High static usually signals restriction or control problems.
distractorExplanations.c: Economizer operation does not directly raise duct static.
distractorExplanations.d: Higher static typically increases fan power.

prompt: Kitchen exhaust and makeup air systems assessed for energy should evaluate:

options.text: Hood operating schedules, makeup air conditioning, and interlocks with cooking loads
Only table setting arrangements
Structural steel camber in the dining room
That makeup air is never conditioned

explanation: Commercial kitchens often have high ventilation-related energy intensity.

distractorExplanations.b: Hood/makeup air interlocks affect HVAC loads materially.
distractorExplanations.c: Structural camber is unrelated to exhaust energy.
distractorExplanations.d: Untreated makeup air can impose large heating/cooling penalties.

prompt: A building with rising EUI year-over-year after occupancy growth should:

options.text: Normalize metrics per occupant or hour of operation before concluding efficiency degradation
Automatically blame envelope insulation without data
Ignore occupancy because only area matters always
Delete prior years of utility data

explanation: Normalization prevents misattributing higher use to worse efficiency.

distractorExplanations.b: Occupancy and hours affect total consumption.
distractorExplanations.c: Some building types scale energy with occupants and runtime.
distractorExplanations.d: Historical trends are essential for diagnosis.

prompt: Demand-controlled ventilation assessment verifies:

options.text: CO₂ or occupancy sensors modulate outdoor air to meet code minimums without over-ventilating
Outdoor air is fixed at maximum design forever
Sensors are disabled to save sensor cost only
Ventilation is unrelated to HVAC energy

explanation: Proper DCV saves fan and conditioning energy while maintaining IAQ.

distractorExplanations.b: DCV reduces ventilation when loads are low.
distractorExplanations.c: Disabled DCV wastes heating/cooling on excess OA.
distractorExplanations.d: Conditioning outdoor air is a major load component.

prompt: Chiller sequencing assessment should confirm:

options.text: Most efficient chiller leads near design load and lag chillers stage off at part load
All chillers run at minimum load simultaneously always
Chiller efficiency is independent of load percentage
Tower fans never interact with chiller performance

explanation: Optimal staging minimizes kW/ton across operating range.

distractorExplanations.b: Poor sequencing wastes energy at part load.
distractorExplanations.c: kW/ton varies with loading and condenser conditions.
distractorExplanations.d: Condenser water temperature affects chiller efficiency.

prompt: Tenant plug load growth in multitenant buildings affects BEAP analysis by:

options.text: Increasing base electric load independent of central HVAC retrofits
Having no measurable effect on utility bills
Eliminating the need for any metering
Replacing all envelope losses

explanation: Assessments must account for tenant vs landlord meter boundaries.

distractorExplanations.b: Plug loads are a major and growing end use.
distractorExplanations.c: Tenant loads complicate allocation and benchmarking.
distractorExplanations.d: Plug loads add to, not replace, HVAC and envelope loads.

prompt: A BEAP evaluating roof insulation upgrade ECMs needs:

options.text: Existing R-value, roof area, heating/cooling degree impacts, and moisture/code constraints
Only the roofing membrane color name
No climate data because roofs are universal
Guaranteed exclusion of any condensation analysis

explanation: Roof insulation ECMs depend on pre-retrofit shell performance and climate.

distractorExplanations.b: Thermal resistance and area drive savings.
distractorExplanations.c: Climate and fuel mix determine insulation value.
distractorExplanations.d: Moisture risk must be considered in envelope upgrades.

prompt: Short-term data logging during assessment can:

options.text: Capture actual runtime, cycling, and load profiles to validate owner-stated schedules
Replace all safety codes for electrical work
Eliminate the need for any utility bill review
Only measure decorative lighting in lobbies

explanation: Portable loggers bridge gaps when permanent metering is unavailable.

distractorExplanations.b: Logging supplements but does not replace code compliance.
distractorExplanations.c: Bill analysis and logging are complementary.
distractorExplanations.d: Loggers are applied to major energy-consuming systems.

prompt: Water-side economizer (strainer cycle) assessment should verify:

options.text: Controls enable free cooling when outdoor wet-bulb allows and prevent uncontrolled tower water migration
Tower water always mixes into chilled water without controls
Water-side economizers work identically in all climates
Chillers must run at full load whenever towers operate

explanation: Proper strainer-cycle control saves chiller energy in suitable climates.

distractorExplanations.b: Uncontrolled mixing can cause health and efficiency problems.
distractorExplanations.c: Wet-bulb conditions determine hours of effectiveness.
distractorExplanations.d: Economizers reduce chiller loading when conditions permit.

prompt: When interviewing facility staff, a BEAP should ask about:

options.text: Comfort complaints, override practices, maintenance budgets, and known equipment problems
Only personal hobbies unrelated to building operation
Guaranteed absence of any comfort issues
Historical stock market performance

explanation: Operator knowledge explains discrepancies between design intent and actual performance.

distractorExplanations.b: Interviews target operational knowledge.
distractorExplanations.c: Complaints reveal control and balancing problems.
distractorExplanations.d: Financial markets are unrelated to facility interviews.

prompt: Motor inventory assessment identifies candidates for:

options.text: Premium efficiency replacements and variable-speed drives on variable-load applications
Immediate replacement of all motors regardless of load or hours
Removal of all motors to eliminate electricity use
Painting motors only without efficiency review

explanation: Motors with high annual run hours and variable torque loads are prime VFD candidates.

distractorExplanations.b: Economics depend on runtime and load factor.
distractorExplanations.c: Motors perform necessary work; upgrades target efficiency.
distractorExplanations.d: Nameplate efficiency and application drive ECM value.

prompt: A BEAP analyzing a hospital should recognize:

options.text: 24/7 operation, high ventilation rates, and critical environmental requirements limit some setback ECMs
Identical operating constraints as unoccupied warehouses
No ventilation-related loads
Mandatory absence of any redundancy

explanation: Sector-specific operations shape feasible ECM portfolios.

distractorExplanations.b: Healthcare has stringent IAQ and reliability requirements.
distractorExplanations.c: Healthcare ventilation energy is substantial.
distractorExplanations.d: Critical facilities require redundancy; assessments respect constraints.

prompt: Renewable or cogeneration systems on site during assessment require:

options.text: Review of production, maintenance, and how generated energy offsets purchased utilities
Ignoring on-site generation in all utility analysis
Assuming infinite output without meter verification
Treating PV as a water conservation measure

explanation: Net site energy metrics must account for self-generation accurately.

distractorExplanations.b: On-site systems affect net consumption and benchmarking.
distractorExplanations.c: Production data should be metered or utility-reconciled.
distractorExplanations.d: PV offsets electricity, not water use.

prompt: Life-cycle cost analysis differs from simple payback by:

options.text: Including time value of money, equipment life, maintenance, and salvage over the analysis period
Using only first cost with no savings
Excluding maintenance forever
Ignoring analysis period length

explanation: LCC supports owner decisions on longer-lived capital ECMs.

distractorExplanations.b: LCC incorporates costs and savings over time.
distractorExplanations.c: Maintenance and replacement are LCC components.
distractorExplanations.d: LCC requires a defined study period.

prompt: An assessment finding open outdoor air dampers in winter suggests:

options.text: Economizer or damper actuator failure causing massive heating load
Optimal free cooling operation in all climates
Improved IAQ with no energy penalty always
Normal VAV minimum flow only

explanation: Stuck dampers are common retrocommissioning fixes with fast payback.

distractorExplanations.b: Open OA dampers in winter waste heating energy.
distractorExplanations.c: Excess OA in extreme weather imposes large conditioning costs.
distractorExplanations.d: Stuck open dampers are faults, not minimum ventilation.

prompt: BEAP professionals should verify compliance with minimum ventilation by referencing:

options.text: ASHRAE Standard 62.1 or applicable local code for outdoor air requirements
Only the building's year of construction with no standard
Structural ACI 318 concrete provisions exclusively
Eliminating outdoor air in all retrofits

explanation: Assessments balance energy savings with maintaining compliant ventilation.

distractorExplanations.b: Ventilation requirements come from recognized standards/codes.
distractorExplanations.c: Structural concrete codes do not set ventilation rates.
distractorExplanations.d: IAQ codes set minimum outdoor air requirements.

prompt: Data center loads assessed within a larger building need:

options.text: Separate IT and cooling energy accounting because PUE and redundancy differ from office loads
Treating IT racks as equivalent to one occupant
Ignoring CRAC/CRAH systems entirely
Assuming no continuous operation

explanation: Mixed-use assessments isolate data center energy for accurate ECM targeting.

distractorExplanations.b: IT loads are orders of magnitude higher per area than people.
distractorExplanations.c: Data center HVAC is specialized and energy-intensive.
distractorExplanations.d: Many data centers operate 24/7.

prompt: A calibrated simulation used in Level II analysis should be:

options.text: Disclosed with assumptions and used to test ECMs, not hidden from the report
Secret so savings cannot be questioned
Run without any comparison to utility data
Discarded after the walk-through only

explanation: Model-based ECM analysis is common in detailed audits when budgets allow.

distractorExplanations.b: Transparency supports owner and financier confidence.
distractorExplanations.c: Calibration to measured data strengthens savings claims.
distractorExplanations.d: Simulation supports measure-level savings estimates in Level II.

prompt: Fuel switching ECMs (e.g., electrification) require BEAP analysis of:

options.text: Marginal energy costs, peak demand impacts, equipment capacity, and carbon policy drivers
Only logo design on utility bills
Ignoring capacity of existing electrical service
Assuming identical COP in all climates without data

explanation: Site vs source and demand charges affect electrification economics.

distractorExplanations.b: Fuel switching needs detailed economic and load analysis.
distractorExplanations.c: Electrification may require service upgrades.
distractorExplanations.d: Heat pump performance varies with climate and load.

prompt: A BEAP should document measurement boundaries when recommending M&V by:

options.text: Stating which meters, systems, and adjustment variables define the savings claim
Promising savings with no baseline period
Avoiding any discussion of interactive effects
Using only anecdotal evidence

explanation: Clear boundaries align with IPMVP and owner financing expectations.

distractorExplanations.b: M&V requires defined baseline and reporting periods.
distractorExplanations.c: Interactive ECM effects should be acknowledged.
distractorExplanations.d: Defensible M&V needs measurable boundaries.

prompt: Warehouse loading dock infiltration during assessment is significant because:

options.text: Open doors introduce large heating/cooling loads and may warrant dock seals or air curtains
Docks are thermally isolated with no airflow exchange
Infiltration only matters in tropical climates
Air curtains always increase energy use with no benefit

explanation: Logistics facilities often show seasonal energy spikes from door operation.

distractorExplanations.b: Open docks exchange large air volumes.
distractorExplanations.c: Cold and hot climates both suffer dock infiltration penalties.
distractorExplanations.d: Proper air curtains reduce infiltration losses.

prompt: ASHRAE BEAP knowledge areas include:

options.text: Only welding certification for duct fabrication
Exclusive focus on refrigerant sales licensing
Structural seismic retrofit design exclusively
Data collection, analysis, reporting, and communication of energy assessment findings

explanation: BEAP spans technical assessment and professional communication per the exam blueprint.

distractorExplanations.a: Fabrication trades are not the BEAP certification focus.
distractorExplanations.b: Refrigerant licensing is separate from BEAP.
distractorExplanations.c: Structural seismic design is outside BEAP scope.

prompt: When existing insulation is damaged by roof leaks, a BEAP should:

options.text: Flag moisture-damaged insulation as both an energy and building-envelope integrity issue
Ignore wet insulation because it still insulates perfectly
Recommend only cosmetic ceiling tile replacement
Increase HVAC cooling setpoints to dry insulation in place only

explanation: Envelope ECMs must address moisture before assuming rated thermal performance.

distractorExplanations.b: Wet insulation loses R-value and risks mold/decay.
distractorExplanations.c: Underlying wet insulation must be remediated.
distractorExplanations.d: Material replacement/drying protocols are needed, not just setpoint changes.

prompt: Peak demand management ECMs assessed may include:

options.text: Battery storage, load shifting, chiller precooling, and demand-limiting controls
Only changing carpet color in corridors
Eliminating all demand charges by definition
Ignoring time-of-use rate structures

explanation: Demand charges can exceed energy charges in some commercial tariffs.

distractorExplanations.b: Demand management targets electrical peak kW.
distractorExplanations.c: ECMs reduce demand; tariffs still apply.
distractorExplanations.d: TOU and demand rates drive demand ECM economics.

prompt: A final BEAP report executive summary should:

options.text: Highlight total savings potential, top measures, and recommended next steps for the owner
Hide all numbers to avoid decision-making
List every light bulb inspected without synthesis
Omit implementation sequencing entirely

explanation: Executive summaries translate technical findings into actionable business cases.

distractorExplanations.b: Executives need concise savings and cost context.
distractorExplanations.c: Summaries synthesize findings for decision makers.
distractorExplanations.d: Phasing helps owners fund and execute ECMs.
